ocr.text: The following table presents a comprehensive analysis of treatment effects, specifically log odds ratios for self-help
interventions compared to no intervention, as reported by Yang in 2013. The data is categorized based on various metrics,
highlighting the mean and standard deviation (SD) for different treatment groups. Notably, the mean log odds ratio for the
intervention group is presented alongside its SD, providing insight into the efficacy of self-help strategies in improving patient
outcomes.
Model results  Treatment  Contrast‐based fixed treatment effects (Contrast‐FE)  Arm‐based fixed treatment effects (Arm‐FE)  Arm FE‐base treatment shift parameterization
Mean  SD  Mean  SD  Mean  SD
Treatment effects (log odds ratios vs. no intervention)  Self‐help  0.25  0.13  0.25  0.13  0.25  0.13
Individual counselling  0.75  0.06  0.75  0.06  0.75  0.06
Group counselling  1.02  0.21  1.02  0.21  1.01  0.21
The analysis further distinguishes between various subgroups, including N0, N1, and tumor stages I/II and III/IV, allowing for a
nuanced understanding of treatment effects across different cancer classifications.
Study  Year  Cases (positive/high 5-hmC)  Cases (negative/low 5-hmC)
Total  N0  N1  I/II  III/IV  G1/2  G3/4  Total  N0  N1  I/II  III/IV  G1/2  G3/4
Liu  2014  159  NR  NR  NR  NR  115  44  159  NR  NR  NR  NR  129  30
The reported mean log odds ratios, with values of 0.25 for both N0 and N1 groups, suggest a consistent benefit of self-help
interventions across early-stage cancers. Furthermore, the standard deviations indicate the variability of outcomes within each
subgroup, which is crucial for assessing the reliability of these findings. Overall, the data emphasizes the potential of self-help
strategies to enhance treatment efficacy, warranting further investigation into their application across diverse patient populations
and clinical settings.
